title: The Energy You Don't See
partLabel: Part One — Respect the Simple Thing

slides.heading:
The Energy
You Don't See
slides.subtitle: Part One — Respect the Simple Thing

slides.label: Scene
slides.text: A maintenance shop. Early morning. Fluorescent lights buzzing to life, casting flat blue-white light across a concrete floor marked with yellow safety lines.

slides.label: Scene
slides.text: A worker picks up the work order. Nothing about this Tuesday morning is unusual. The work order says bearing replacement. Twenty-minute job. He's done it a hundred times.

slides.label: Scene
slides.text: He presses the stop button. The belt slows. Stops. Opens an access panel and starts loosening a bolt. He pressed the stop button. The belt isn't moving. So he's working.

slides.heading: Ten feet away
slides.body: The electrical disconnect is in the OFF position. But there's no lock on it. No tag. Just an unprotected switch that anyone could flip.

slides.heading:
He stopped
the machine.
He didn't secure it.
slides.body: There's a difference. And the difference is everything.

slides.heading: A padlock on a breaker
slides.body: Here's what most people think lockout/tagout is: you put a padlock on a breaker before you work on a machine. Simple procedure. Common sense.

slides.heading:
Lockout/tagout isn't
a procedure.
It's a barrier.
slides.body: The only barrier between you and the energy stored in the machine you're about to put your hands inside.

slides.label: Five Types of Hazardous Energy
slides.boxText: When someone says 'hazardous energy,' most people think electricity. But OSHA recognizes five types.
slides.body: If you only think about one of them, the other four can kill you while you're busy being careful about electricity.

slides.heading: The one you know
slides.body: Current flowing through conductors. But even after you cut the power, capacitors in motors and control boards can discharge through you in a fraction of a second.

slides.heading: Pressurized systems
slides.body: Two thousand PSI in a hydraulic line. Over a hundred times the pressure in your car tires. Close the valve and that pressure doesn't vanish. It's still waiting.

slides.heading:
Springs. Flywheels.
Compression.
slides.body: A compressed spring doesn't care whether the machine is on or off. It's loaded. It wants to release. If you're between the spring and where it wants to go, the machine being 'off' doesn't help you.

slides.heading: Anything suspended
slides.body: A press platen held up by hydraulic pressure. Gravity is constant and patient. The moment the thing holding that weight fails, everything falls. And gravity doesn't give warnings.

slides.heading: Reactive substances
slides.body: Supply lines with corrosives, solvents, gases. Even steam. A residual chemical in a line you assumed was empty. A valve that didn't fully close.

slides.heading:
Five energy sources.
One workspace.
slides.body: This is what's inside the machine you're about to service. Not one energy source. Potentially all five. Each one requires its own specific method of isolation and verification.

slides.label: Off vs. Safe
slides.boxText: What does a machine look like when it's off? Quiet. Still. Nothing moving. To every sense you have, this machine is safe.
slides.body: But 'off' and 'safe' are not the same word. 'Off' is a button you push. 'Safe' is a process you complete.

slides.label: Stored Energy
slides.boxText: The energy that remains in a system after you've isolated it from its source.
slides.body: You closed the valve. Good. New energy can't enter. But the energy already in the line is still there. Isolation stops new energy. It doesn't remove what's present.

items.text: Prepare: Identify all energy sources using machine-specific procedures
Shutdown: Use normal stopping procedures, verify all movement stopped
Isolate: Disconnect from every energy source — not just electrical
Lock: Apply physical barriers that prevent re-energization
Stored Energy: Address what's left — bleed, block, discharge, release
Verify: Try to start the machine — silence is proof

slides.heading:
Simple feels
skippable
slides.body: The procedure is simple. Six steps. A padlock. A tag. And that simplicity is exactly what makes it dangerous. Simple feels like something you can shortcut.

slides.label: Back to Tuesday
slides.text: Same worker. Same work order. But now, before walking to the conveyor, he reaches for the lockout station. Pulls a yellow padlock from its hook.

slides.label: The Difference
slides.text: The electrical disconnect, locked. A tag filled out and hanging. The worker presses the start button. Nothing happens. Verification. The machine is safe — not just off.

slides.heading:
Two minutes.
Four ounces.
slides.body: That's the difference between the five types of energy inside that machine and the person who reaches inside it. Everyone goes home safe.

slides.heading: Module 1 — Key Concepts
items: Lockout/tagout is a barrier between you and hazardous energy — not just a procedure
Five types of hazardous energy: electrical, hydraulic/pneumatic, mechanical, gravitational, chemical
'Off' and 'safe' are not the same thing — off is a button, safe is a process
Stored energy remains after isolation — the energy already in the system doesn't disappear
The six-step sequence exists because each step addresses a different failure mode
The simplicity of the procedure is what makes it dangerous — simple feels skippable

quiz.question: A maintenance worker turns off a machine using the stop button and the machine is no longer moving. This means:
options: The machine is safe to work on
The machine is off, but 'off' and 'safe' are not the same thing
The machine only needs a lock on the main breaker before work begins

quiz.question: After closing the supply valve on a hydraulic line, the pressure gauge still shows 2000 PSI. This is because:
options: The valve is faulty and needs to be replaced
The gauge is broken and showing an incorrect reading
Closing the valve stops new energy from entering, but the energy already in the line remains
Hydraulic systems always show residual pressure that isn't dangerous

quiz.question: Sarah needs to replace a roller bearing on a conveyor system. The conveyor has an electrical motor, a hydraulic tensioning system, and a spring-loaded guard. How many types of hazardous energy does she need to consider?
options: One — electrical, since that's what powers the motor
She should check all five types; even if only three are obvious, gravitational and chemical sources may also be present

quiz.question: The six-step lockout sequence exists because:
options: OSHA requires documentation of each step for compliance
Each step addresses a different aspect of making equipment safe — skipping any one leaves a gap in protection
The steps are a simplified version of a more complex engineering process
Multiple steps make it harder for employees to skip the procedure entirely
